## 7\.6 Carbon Storage Implications of Ada Soil Findings

The Ada\-Songor soil data cannot be read in isolation from the water quality conditions documented in Chapter 6\. The two datasets describe a system under simultaneous stress at multiple ecological levels, and the interaction between water column conditions and soil conditions is where the most significant carbon storage risks are concentrated\.

### Implications for the Carbon Inventory Report

__Active carbon loss at Pute\.__ The combination of near\-acid\-sulphate pH, depleted humus, high surface temperature, and strongly oxidising water column conditions indicates that Pute lagoon is an active carbon\-loss location within the monitoring baseline period\. Any carbon accounting that assigns a stable or historical average humus value to the Pute stations will overstate the carbon stock retained at this location\. A conservative accounting approach would treat Pute's current humus values as an upper bound on residual carbon stock and model expected further loss under continued degradation conditions\.

__Potential carbon loss acceleration from nutrient enrichment\.__ The extreme N at Wasakuse \(984 mg/kg\) and extreme P and K at Obane \(705 and 1,280 mg/kg respectively\) represent enrichment conditions that the standard soil carbon accounting framework does not directly incorporate as a loss risk\. The Carbon Inventory Report should flag these stations as subject to nutrient\-stimulated decomposition risk and should apply a conservative treatment of the Obane humus value \(26\.0%\) pending two to three quarters of monitoring data that can establish whether it is stable or declining\.

__Recovery trajectory at Obane\.__ Conversely, the Obane restored mangrove data provides a defensible recovery reference for the Ada system\. The 26\.0% humus, near\-neutral pH, and 34\.2°C soil temperature at Obane represent conditions that are consistent with active carbon accumulation under a functioning restored mangrove canopy\. The Carbon Inventory Report can use the Obane baseline as the reference condition for what a successfully restored Ada mangrove site achieves at an intermediate stage of recovery, a reference point that anchors the modelled carbon benefit of any planned restoration at Pute or adjacent degraded sites\.

Taken together, the Ada soil data establishes that the Songor system is not a uniform landscape with a single carbon storage trajectory\. It is a mosaic of active carbon loss at Pute, potential carbon loss acceleration at Wasakuse\-Obane from nutrient enrichment, and demonstrable carbon recovery at the Obane restored site\. Each zone requires a distinct treatment within the Carbon Inventory framework, and each zone requires the quarterly monitoring cadence established under the MRV system to track whether conditions are improving, stable, or worsening, because at Ada\-Songor, the answer is different in every corner of the landscape\.

__Chapter 8__

__Integrated Ecosystem Health Condition Assessment__

# Chapter 8: Integrated Ecosystem Health Condition Assessment

## 8\.0 Chapter Overview

This chapter is the analytical summary of the Standalone Ecosystem Health Monitoring Report\. Its purpose is to transform the parameter\-by\-parameter findings of Chapters 4 through 7 into a single, coherent management decision tool: a condition score for every monitoring cluster across both sites, linked explicitly to carbon stock integrity, and translated into a prioritised action framework that MLNR, MEST, and the Wildlife Division can use directly\.

## 8\.1 Condition Assessment Framework

### 8\.1\.1 The Logic of a Four\-Tier System

Most ecological assessment frameworks employ a three\-tier classification, Good, Moderate, and Poor because this maps conveniently onto traffic\-light communication for non\-specialist audiences\. This report uses four tiers: __Good__, __Moderate__, __Stressed__, and __Alert__\. The reason for the additional tier is specific to the Ghana Blue Carbon context and the data from the field assessment\.

### 8\.1\.2 Tier Definitions and Quantitative Criteria

__GOOD__ \- Ecosystem function within optimal or near\-optimal ranges\. All monitored parameters consistent with conditions that actively support mangrove productivity and soil organic carbon accumulation\. Carbon sequestration conditions fully maintained\. Standard monitoring cadence appropriate\.

*Water quality criteria:* pH 6\.0–8\.0; salinity within species tolerance \(5–35 ppt for *Rhizophora*; up to 90 ppt for *Avicennia* under natural conditions\); ORP between −200 and \+300 mV \(reducing to mildly oxidising\); DO >5 mg/L; no anomalous nutrient or conductivity loading\.

*Soil quality criteria:* Humus >25%; moisture >25%; soil pH 5\.5–7\.5; nitrate and phosphate within natural mangrove\-soil range; potassium supportive of productivity; no acid sulphate indicators\.

*Vegetation and carbon:* Mangrove canopy intact\. SOC accumulation conditions maintained at benchmark levels\. No management intervention required beyond standard protection and monitoring\.

__MODERATE__ \- Ecosystem function showing measurable stress signals, but core function not yet impaired\. One or more parameters outside optimal range but not exceeding critical thresholds\. Carbon sequestration likely continuing but at reduced rate relative to benchmark\. Management attention and monitoring intensification required; passive intervention or trend surveillance may be sufficient at this stage\.

*Water quality criteria:* pH 5\.5–6\.0 or 8\.0–8\.5 \(approaching but not crossing critical boundaries\); salinity at upper end of optimal range or slightly exceeding it; ORP \+300 to \+600 mV \(moderately oxidising\); DO 3–5 mg/L; conductivity or TDS elevated above benchmark but not at critical levels\.

*Soil quality criteria:* Humus 15–25%; moisture 20–25% \(at or approaching the anaerobic threshold\); soil pH approaching 5\.5 or above 7\.5; nutrients at sub\-optimal or marginal levels\.

*Vegetation and carbon:* Mangrove canopy partially degraded or vegetation under stress\. Carbon sequestration likely continuing but reduced\. SOC stable for now, but trend monitoring essential\. Restoration feasibility assessable with additional data\.

__STRESSED__ \- Core ecosystem function impaired\. Multiple parameters outside acceptable ranges, or one parameter at or near a defined critical threshold\. Carbon storage conditions compromised, existing stocks may be at risk of mineralisation or destabilisation\. Active management intervention required, passive monitoring insufficient\.

*Water quality criteria:* pH <5\.5 or >8\.5; salinity consistently above species optimal range \(>40 ppt for *Rhizophora* or persistently hypersaline\); ORP \+600 to \+800 mV \(strongly oxidising\); DO approaching 3 mg/L or below during monitoring; conductivity or TDS significantly above benchmark\.

*Soil quality criteria:* Humus <15%; moisture consistently below 20%; soil pH below 5\.5 or above 8\.0; nutrient depletion or enrichment at levels interfering with biological function; acid sulphate indicators present\.

*Vegetation and carbon:* Mangrove canopy significantly degraded or absent\. Carbon storage conditions compromised, current stocks at meaningful risk\. Active site management, community engagement, and hydrological assessment required\.

__ALERT__ \- Immediate management action required\. One or more parameters at catastrophic or irreversible\-risk levels\. Carbon storage at direct and current risk\. Delay in intervention will result in irreversible ecological and carbon losses that cannot be recovered on any management\-relevant timescale\.

## 8\.2 Integrated Condition Summary Table

The following table presents the master assessment summary across all 13 ecological assessment zones in the Ghana Blue Carbon monitoring network\. Condition scores use the four\-tier classification defined in Section 8\.1\. Carbon Stock Integrity ratings use a four\-tier scale: High / Medium / At Risk / Critical\. Priority actions are condensed to their operational core, the single most time\-sensitive management requirement at each zone\.

__Chapter 9__

__Monitoring Framework and Operational Action Plan__

# Chapter 9: Monitoring Framework and Operational Action Plan

## 9\.1 From Baseline to Monitoring Programme: The Strategic Shift

The December 2025 survey did what a baseline is supposed to do: it documented the condition of the ecosystem at a defined moment in time, with sufficient spatial resolution and parameter coverage to establish the ecological benchmarks against which future change will be measured\. 

A baseline without a monitoring programme is a photograph with no film in the camera\. It records a moment but cannot tell you whether the next moment is better or worse\. Ghana's most important blue carbon landscape contains an acid sulphate emergency at Anyanui, a hypersaline stress zone at Dzita, a geochemical anomaly at the Ada interior lagoon, and a deforestation trajectory of 2\.15 percent per year across the site that contains 69 percent of the nation's coastal mangroves\. 

Understanding whether any of those conditions is improving, stable, or worsening requires a system that generates comparable data from the same locations across successive time periods\. The December 2025 baseline established the reference coordinates\. 

The monitoring programme described in this chapter translates the georeferenced infrastructure, trained community capacity, and calibrated instrumentation into an operational system: 80 stations across two Ramsar sites, measured quarterly, against defined alert thresholds, with an escalation protocol that routes ecological signals to the institutions with the authority to act on them\.

## 9\.2 Quarterly Schedule and Seasonal Logic

### 9\.3\.1 Why Quarterly: Not Monthly, Not Annual

The quarterly monitoring cadence is not arbitrary; it is calibrated to the ecological dynamics of the two systems and to the operational capacity of the CCA network that will conduct the surveys\.

Monthly monitoring would generate twelve datasets per year per station\. The analytical capacity required to process, validate, and interpret 80 × 15 parameter readings across twelve time points annually would exceed the current institutional capacity of both the district Wildlife Division and the national MRV platform\. More importantly, ecological variation in these systems at the monthly timescale is dominated by weather noise, a single unseasonal rainfall event can temporarily depress salinity by several parts per thousand at a given station; a calm, cloudless week can elevate surface water temperature by 2–3°C\. These short\-cycle fluctuations are real but are not the signals the monitoring system is designed to detect\. They would require constant interpretation and, without the analytical infrastructure to contextualise them, would generate false escalation alerts that erode institutional confidence in the system\.

Annual monitoring \(a single survey per year\) would cost almost nothing in operational terms but would provide almost no ecological intelligence\. The December 2025 baseline itself demonstrated why: it was conducted during the dry season and yet recorded unseasonal rainfall effects at several stations, demonstrating that a single annual snapshot cannot characterise even the seasonal behaviour of these systems, let alone detect the sub\-seasonal anomalies \(pollution spikes, acute hypersalinity events, dissolved oxygen crashes during the hottest dry\-season weeks\) that represent the most ecologically consequential signals\.

Quarterly monitoring captures the four ecologically distinct phases of the annual cycle that govern mangrove function and carbon dynamics in coastal Ghana\. The dry season brings maximum thermal stress, maximum evaporative concentration of dissolved solids, minimum freshwater input from the Volta, and the conditions under which acid sulphate chemistry advances most aggressively when tidal exchange is disrupted\. The late\-dry\-season transition sees salinity begin to decline as the first rains arrive, but water temperatures remain high and dissolved oxygen can be depressed by the warm, stratified conditions before mixing events restore the DO profile\. The wet season brings freshwater dilution that can drop salinity by 10–15 ppt at the most Volta\-proximate stations, reduces temperatures, and triggers the highest biological productivity across the mangrove stands, this is the season in which the *Rhizophora racemosa* canopy is at its most photosynthetically active and in which soil organic matter inputs from leaf fall are beginning to accumulate\. The wet\-to\-dry transition is the season of maximum hydrological instability: some years the rains end abruptly; in others, a second rainfall pulse modifies what would otherwise be the expected salinity and conductivity trajectory\. Quarterly monitoring resolves all four phases and builds the temporal pattern against which anomaly detection operates\.

### 9\.3\.2 Quarterly Survey Rounds: Schedule Table

Round

Target Months

Seasonal Phase

Primary Ecological Focus

Expected Parameter Behaviour

Round 1

December–January

Peak dry season

Thermal and salinity stress maxima; acid sulphate risk at Anyanui; ORP monitoring at Ada interior

Maximum salinity and conductivity; elevated temperatures; potential DO depression at most exposed stations; Anyanui pH at or near baseline minimum

Round 2

March–April

Late\-dry / early\-wet transition

Salinity decline onset; freshwater arrival monitoring; Volta pulse detection at Azizanya\-Ayigbo

Declining salinity and conductivity at Volta\-proximate stations; temperature beginning to fall; DO partial recovery; first wet\-season soil moisture increase at Aborlorvi

Round 3

June–July

Peak wet season

Maximum freshwater dilution; peak mangrove productivity; soil carbon preservation assessment

Minimum salinity at freshwater\-proximate stations; lower temperatures; DO at annual maximum; humus and moisture values at seasonal peak at Galotse and Genui

Round 4

September–October

Wet\-to\-dry transition

Freshwater withdrawal; salinity rebound; hydrological stability monitoring

Rising salinity and conductivity; evaporative concentration beginning; soil moisture declining from wet\-season peak; Aborlorvi moisture interrogation for restoration feasibility determination

Fieldwork for each round should be completed within a 14\-day window to minimise within\-round temporal variability\. At Keta, fieldwork typically requires 5–7 field days given the 57\-station coverage across the 530 km² complex: at Ada, 2–3 days for 23 stations\. The order of station visits within each round must be consistent across rounds, visiting stations in the same sequence prevents the systematic time\-of\-day bias that can otherwise inflate apparent within\-round variability at stations visited in the afternoon, when water temperatures are elevated, versus early morning\.

## 9\.4 Alert Thresholds and Escalation Protocol

### 9\.4\.1 Parameter Alert Thresholds

The monitoring system operates against a defined set of alert thresholds for both water quality and soil parameters\. These thresholds are set at values that represent ecologically critical boundaries, levels at or beyond which the ecosystem's capacity to sustain carbon sequestration or mangrove productivity is directly and materially compromised\. They are not aspirational targets\. They are the lines that, when crossed, require an institutional response to occur within a defined timeframe\.

__Water quality alert thresholds:__

*pH below 4\.0 or above 9\.0 \- Immediate escalation\. A pH below 4\.0 indicates active acid sulphate chemistry in contact with the water column; a pH above 9\.0 indicates extreme alkalisation incompatible with mangrove physiology\. The pH 2\.7 reading at Anyanui\-Tunu road during the December 2025 baseline already exceeds this threshold and is under active Alert protocol \(see Section 10\.4\.3\)\.*

*Dissolved oxygen \(DO\) below 3\.0 mg/L \- Immediate escalation\. At DO below 3 mg/L, most aquatic fauna are in hypoxic stress; below 2 mg/L, the water column is effectively anoxic\. Sustained low DO in the water column adjacent to mangrove stands indicates organic pollution, algal bloom, or the collapse of the aerobic decomposition cycle that supports nutrient availability for mangrove roots\.*

*Salinity above 50 ppt \- Immediate escalation\. At 50 ppt, both dominant Keta mangrove species \(Rhizophora racemosa and Avicennia germinans\) are outside their physiological tolerance range\. Even Avicennia, the more salt\-tolerant species, shows growth suppression above 45 ppt\. A reading above 50 ppt signals a hydrological crisis \- either the complete isolation of a lagoon compartment from freshwater input or an extreme evaporative concentration event incompatible with mangrove survival\.*

*ORP sustained above \+600 mV in water column \- Escalation required\. ORP above \+600 mV indicates a strongly oxidising geochemical environment hostile to the reducing conditions that preserve soil organic carbon\. The Ada\-Songor interior lagoon readings of 832–1,547 mV already exceed this threshold\. ORP values above 600 mV in water overlying mangrove or wetland sediments mean that the geochemical barrier that suppresses aerobic decomposition of the carbon pool is absent, and the organic matter is being actively oxidised\.*

__Soil parameter alert thresholds:__

- *Nitrate above 500 mg/kg at mangrove\-proximate stations \- Escalation required\. This represents the MRV system's eutrophication alert level, established from the Ada\-Songor baseline data where 984 mg/kg was recorded at Wasakuse\. At nitrate concentrations above 500 mg/kg, nitrogen\-stimulated microbial decomposition of soil organic matter becomes a meaningful risk \- the nutrient enrichment that promotes short\-term plant growth also accelerates the breakdown of the humus pool that constitutes the site's blue carbon asset\.*
- *Soil pH below 4\.5 \- Immediate escalation\. This threshold indicates the onset of confirmed acid sulphate soil conditions in the soil profile itself, not merely in the adjacent water column\. It signals that the acid front has penetrated from the water surface into the organic matter layer, placing the humus pool under direct chemical attack\.*

__Cross\-parameter trend alert:__

- *Any parameter exceeding the December 2025 baseline value by more than 30% in two consecutive quarterly rounds \- Trend alert requiring escalation review\. This threshold is designed to catch progressive deterioration that does not yet trigger individual\-parameter alerts but represents a directional ecological change that requires investigation before it crosses a critical threshold\. A 30% increase in salinity over two consecutive rounds at a station where the December 2025 value was 25 ppt, for example, would bring that station to 32\.5 ppt \- not yet at the critical threshold, but on a trajectory that, if continued for another round, would approach it\.*

### 9\.4\.2 Standard Escalation Protocol

When a field team records a reading at or beyond any alert threshold, the following protocol applies regardless of the monitoring round in progress:

__*Step 1 \- Alert reading recorded\.*__* CCA or field team records the anomalous value in the MRV Mobile App \(highly recommended\) or Data Sheet, flags it using the app's alert function or via a telephone call, and photographs the station and water or soil condition at the time of measurement\. This is completed on the day of the field reading\.*

__*Step 2 \- 14\-day field verification visit\.*__* Within 14 days of the alert reading, the monitoring team returns to the flagged station for an independent verification measurement\. The purpose of this visit is to distinguish between an instrument calibration error, a one\-off acute event that has already resolved, and a confirmed sustained anomaly\. If the verification reading confirms the anomaly \(either at or beyond the original alert threshold\) the station moves to the confirmed\-alert category\. If the verification reading is within normal range, the original reading is documented as a potential instrument error or one\-off event and the station returns to standard monitoring\.*

__*Step 3 \- Confirmed anomaly report\.*__* If the verification visit confirms a sustained anomaly, the CCA or district field officer prepares a formal confirmed\-anomaly report and submits it to the district Wildlife Division within 30 days of the original alert reading\. The report must include: the station ID and GPS coordinates, the original alert reading with date and time, the verification reading with date and time, photographs from both visits, the field officer's assessment of the likely cause, and a recommendation for management response\.*

__*Step 4 \- Wildlife Division response window\.*__* The district Wildlife Division has 60 days from receipt of the confirmed anomaly report to: \(a\) identify the probable cause of the anomaly and initiate or commission a management response, or \(b\) determine that the anomaly does not require a management intervention at this time and document the reasoning for that determination\. Either response must be formally recorded in the MRV platform\.*

__*Step 5 \- National escalation\.*__* If the district Wildlife Division has not formally responded within 60 days of receiving the confirmed anomaly report, or if the identified cause requires a management response beyond the authority or resource capacity of the district tier, the issue escalates to MLNR and MEST\. National\-level escalation is also required automatically for any anomaly involving acid sulphate formation, active vegetation die\-back combined with anomalous chemistry, or any combination of parameters suggesting an acute pollution event of off\-site origin\.*

## 9\.5 Community Change Agent \(CCA\) Operational Role

### 9\.5\.1 CCA Mandate and Training Foundation

Community Change Agents are the operational backbone of the quarterly monitoring network\. Their training occurred in the field, alongside the scientific team, during the December 2025 baseline campaign, this was not a classroom induction but a hands\-on demonstration of sampling methodology, instrument operation, and data recording at the actual monitoring stations\. CCAs handled the Apera SX751 and Portable Terminal 4002\-BXSZD instruments during the baseline survey and are familiar with the station locations, the parameter ranges expected at their designated clusters, and the physical landscape and access routes they will navigate in subsequent rounds\.

Each of the 13 monitoring clusters has one or more designated CCAs drawn from the adjacent community or communities\. This geographic assignment is intentional: CCAs with local knowledge of a specific cluster are better placed to distinguish normal ecological conditions from genuine anomalies, to recognise changes in vegetation condition, and to identify anthropogenic activities that may be the proximate drivers of parameter change\. They are not simply data collectors; they are locally embedded ecological observers whose contextual knowledge amplifies the signal value of the instrument readings they record\.

If instruments different from those used during the December 2025 baseline are introduced for future monitoring rounds, CCAs must receive updated training specifically on those instruments before the round in which they are deployed\. The quality of the instrument data depends entirely on correct calibration and operation; introducing a new device without training undermines the comparability of the entire dataset\.

### 9\.5\.2 Quarterly Field Procedures

CCAs operating under the quarterly monitoring schedule are responsible for the following activities at each round:

__Water quality sampling:__ Using the Apera SX751, CCAs measure pH, temperature, electrical conductivity, salinity, total dissolved solids, dissolved oxygen, ORP, and resistivity at each of their designated water quality stations\. Measurements are taken at the same sub\-location within each station \(defined by the GPS coordinates and field description established in December 2025\), at a consistent depth \(surface to 30 cm, or mid\-water column in shallow lagoon settings\), and recorded with the date, time, station ID, GPS coordinates, and the device model and last calibration date\.

__Soil sampling:__ Using the Portable Terminal 4002\-BXSZD, CCAs collect nitrate, phosphate, potassium, pH, moisture, humus, and temperature readings at designated soil sampling stations\. Soil samples are taken from the established sampling depth \(0–20 cm surface horizon\) at the GPS\-recorded locations used in December 2025\. Where the instrument requires insertion into undisturbed soil, CCAs must clear surface debris before insertion to prevent contamination of the reading by decomposed litter\.

__Data upload:__ All field readings are entered into a field toolkit \(sheets, pens,\) or an MRV Mobile App after each field session\. Data should be uploaded before the CCA leaves the field area or, at the latest, on the evening of the sampling day if they use an App\. Delayed upload increases the risk of data loss and deprives the district validation tier of timely access to field records\.

__Alert notification:__ If any reading meets or approaches an alert threshold during the field session, the CCA activates the informs the District Wildlife Officer via telephone call or the app's alert function, which generates an immediate notification to the district Wildlife Division officer\. This alert function bypasses the standard quarterly reporting cycle and ensures that anomalous readings trigger a rapid institutional response without waiting for the end\-of\-round data compilation\. Concurrent with the digital alert, the CCA photographs the sampling location \(water surface, soil condition, vegetation condition, and any visible anthropogenic disturbance\) and uploads the photographs alongside the parameter reading\.

__Anomaly documentation:__ Where a CCA observes conditions that are not captured by instrument measurements \(a dead mangrove stand previously not present, new infrastructure encroachment, visible oil sheen or discolouration on water, cattle damage to wetland margins\) they record a qualitative field observation note in the app or their notepad\. These qualitative observations are as ecologically significant as the quantitative readings in many cases\.

### 9\.5\.3 CCA Station Access and Governance Implications

Access to monitoring stations is not only a logistical matter; it is a governance indicator\. Whether CCAs can consistently reach their designated stations directly reflects the operational health of the community governance structure underpinning the monitoring programme\.

A station that becomes inaccessible because a land\-use conflict has escalated, because a road construction project has blocked a wetland access route, or because community relations with the monitoring programme have broken down is not merely an operational inconvenience\. It is a Layer III MRV signal, a governance and safeguard indicator that the institutional conditions required to sustain monitoring at that location have deteriorated\. The Wildlife Division, when reviewing quarterly data submission records, should track not only the parameter values recorded but also the completeness of station coverage\. Any station that is not visited in a given quarterly round requires an explanation, whether the gap resulted from equipment failure, field team absence, or access restriction\. The distinction matters: equipment failure is a logistical problem solvable by maintenance and spare inventory; access restriction is a governance problem requiring institutional attention from the district Wildlife Division, the MMDA, and potentially MLNR\.

The formalization of CCA roles including compensation arrangements, defined institutional relationships with the Wildlife Division, and documented recognition within CREMA governance structures is a prerequisite for maintaining the 80\-station network beyond the initial few rounds of the programme\. CCAs who conducted the December 2025 baseline were motivated by training, participation, and the novelty of the programme; sustaining that motivation across multi\-year quarterly cycles requires that their role carries formal institutional recognition, predictable operational support, and a tangible connection to the outcomes the programme is designed to achieve\.

### 9\.7\.2 Trend Analysis and the Three\-Year Milestone

A single quarterly dataset can confirm the baseline and detect acute anomalies\. Two quarterly datasets can suggest a directional trend\. After three years of quarterly monitoring \(twelve rounds of data at each of the 80 stations\) the dataset will have sufficient temporal depth to formally separate seasonal oscillation from structural ecological change\. The three\-year trend analysis will answer the questions that the December 2025 baseline cannot answer alone: Is salinity at the Anyanui corridor declining, stable, or rising? Is the ORP anomaly in the Ada interior lagoon varying seasonally or is it a fixed, structural feature of the system? Is humus accumulation continuing at the Galotse benchmark stations, or is there evidence of the first declines in soil organic matter?

These trend assessments are not academic exercises\. They feed directly into three operational outputs: the annual national GHG inventory submitted by Ghana under its Nationally Determined Contribution \(NDC\) commitments, which uses the ecological condition data to contextualise carbon stock status; carbon verification site visits by Verra, Gold Standard, or Plan Vivo auditors, who will review the quarterly dataset as evidence of continued ecological integrity at the monitored sites; and adaptive management decisions at the district and national tier, including when to escalate interventions from monitoring and CCA engagement to active management responses such as hydrological engineering at Anyanui, cattle exclusion at Obane, or restoration planting at Aborlorvi\.

### 9\.7\.3 Backups and Data Integrity

All field data sheets must be completed with the standard information set: site name, GPS coordinates, descriptive notes on site condition, date, time, recorder's name, and equipment model and calibration status\. Any deliberate variation from the standard sampling protocol must be recorded and communicated to the district Wildlife Division\. Silent variations destroy comparability with the baseline record and with other quarterly rounds\. The data will be stored and archived as part of the National Blue Carbon MRV System\.

## 9\.8 Equipment Standardisation and Maintenance

### 9\.8\.1 Instrument Continuity as a Scientific Requirement

The scientific value of the quarterly monitoring dataset depends entirely on the comparability of measurements across rounds\. A dissolved oxygen reading of 5\.2 mg/L recorded by the Apera SX751 in December 2025 is directly comparable to a reading of 5\.4 mg/L recorded by the same model in June 2026, if both instruments were calibrated against the same reference solutions before the survey\. That same June 2026 reading recorded by a different instrument model \(even one marketed as equivalent\) is not directly comparable without a validated cross\-calibration exercise that documents the offset between instruments and applies a correction factor\. Cross\-calibrations are technically feasible but operationally burdensome and introduce their own sources of uncertainty\.

The default position of the monitoring programme is therefore instrument continuity: the Apera SX751 multiparameter water testing device for water quality, and the Portable Terminal 4002\-BXSZD Ver 1\.0 for soil chemistry, are the designated instruments for all 80 stations across all quarterly rounds, maintained in serviceable condition and replaced like\-for\-like if they reach end of operational life\. Any departure from this standard requires a formal protocol variation notice submitted to the district Wildlife Division and recorded in the MRV platform, with documentation of the cross\-calibration exercise conducted before the variant instrument is used in the field\.

### 9\.8\.2 Calibration Protocol

Before each quarterly survey round meaning before fieldwork begins, not during or after both instruments must be calibrated against certified reference solutions\. For the Apera SX751, calibration must cover at minimum the pH, conductivity/salinity, and DO channels, using reference solutions within their certified validity window\. Calibration drift in any channel should be documented and, if it exceeds the instrument's stated accuracy specification, reported as a data quality flag on all readings taken after the last confirmed calibration point\.

For the Portable Terminal 4002\-BXSZD, the calibration protocol specified in the instrument manual must be followed for each soil parameter channel\. The soil instrument's quick\-test platform relies on insert probes that are sensitive to soil contamination; probes must be cleaned between station insertions according to the manufacturer's specification to prevent carryover error between sites\.

Calibration records \(the date, time, reference solution batch number, pre\-calibration reading, post\-calibration reading, and the name of the technician performing the calibration\) must be recorded in the pre\-survey calibration log and uploaded to the MRV platform as metadata associated with the corresponding quarterly dataset\. These records are audit requirements for carbon market verification and must be maintained throughout the programme\.

### 9\.8\.3 Equipment Inventory and Spare Instruments

The monitoring programme must budget for the full lifecycle cost of instrument maintenance, not only the cost of instrument procurement\. Sensor replacement for the Apera SX751 \(particularly the DO membrane, pH electrode, and conductivity cell\) is required on the manufacturer's recommended service interval, which varies by use intensity and storage conditions\. In a tropical coastal environment, electrode degradation is faster than in laboratory settings; annual sensor replacement for heavily used instruments is a realistic planning assumption\.

It is strongly recommended that one complete spare set of monitoring instruments \(one Apera SX751 and one Portable Terminal 4002\-BXSZD\) be maintained at each of the two sites \(Keta and Ada\), held under the custody of the district Wildlife Division office\. These spare sets serve two functions: they enable immediate instrument substitution if a field instrument fails mid\-survey, preventing the loss of an entire quarterly round's data; and they provide the reference instruments against which field instruments can be cross\-checked if calibration anomalies are suspected\. The spare instrument held at the Wildlife Division office must itself be maintained in calibrated condition, a spare that has never been calibrated and has been sitting on a shelf for twelve months is not a reliable backup\.

Calibration consumables \(reference solutions, electrode storage solutions, cleaning materials\) must be included in the annual programme budget as recurring items, not as one\-off procurement costs\. Underfunding of consumables is one of the most common failure modes of community\-based monitoring programmes in analogous regional contexts: instruments are procured, CCAs are trained, surveys begin and then field calibration becomes approximate or ceases entirely because no budget provision was made for the reference solutions required to verify instrument accuracy before each round\.

__Chapter 10__

__Conclusions and Management Recommendations__

# Chapter 10: Conclusions and Management Recommendations

## 10\.0 Chapter Overview

This chapter closes the Standalone Ecosystem Health Monitoring Report by translating the findings of Chapters 4 through 8 into a structured, institutionally grounded management programme\. 

Recommendations are grounded in the field evidence, not in generic ecological management principles\. 

## 10\.1 Management Recommendations \- From Baseline to Operational Monitoring

### 10\.3\.1 Implementation Requirements for Operational Monitoring

- *Adopt the quarterly survey calendar defined in Chapter 9 across all 13 assessment zones, anchored on the four seasonal windows \(late dry, onset wet, peak wet, early dry\)\.*
- *Confirm standing agreements with the Keta Municipal Assembly and Ada East District Assembly so that CCAs have formal authority to access stations and report findings into the assembly environmental health desk\.*
- *Lock in the Apera SX751 \(water\) and Portable Terminal 4002\-BXSZD \(soil\) as the two reference instruments for the full five\-year monitoring cycle, instrument continuity is a scientific precondition for trend detection\.*

### 10\.3\.2 Capacity Building \- Community Change Agents and District Staff

Community Change Agents \(CCAs\) are the operational backbone of this monitoring programme\. District environmental and fisheries officers are the escalation layer\. Both groups need structured, funded, recurring training, not one\-off workshops\.

__Audience__

__Number__

__Initial training__

__Refresher cadence__

__Core competencies__

Community Change Agents

26 \(2 per cluster × 13 zones\)

5 days residential

2 days every 6 months

Instrument use, station access, data recording, escalation protocol, first\-line community engagement

Cluster Leads \(senior CCAs\)

13 \(1 per zone\)

7 days residential \+ field shadow

3 days quarterly

QA on CCA submissions, field calibration, data upload, incident reporting

District Technical Focal Points

4 \(Keta Municipal × 2, Ada East × 2\)

3 days technical

1 day quarterly

Interpretation of tier changes, coordination with Wildlife Division, assembly reporting

GAB Monitoring Coordinator

1

Already in post

Continuous mentoring

Programme oversight, trend analysis, Ramsar reporting, donor communication

__*Year 1 training cost envelope \(indicative\): 26 CCAs \+ 13 Cluster Leads initial training, four quarterly refreshers, per\-diems and facilitation ≈ USD 200,000\)\. District focal\-point training ≈ USD 100,000\. Total capacity\-building line: ≈ USD 250,000 in Year 1, stepping down to USD 75,000/year in Years 2–5*__*\.*

### 10\.3\.4 Digital Platform \- The Webapp / Mobile App Requirement

A webapp plus companion mobile app is not optional\. Paper forms alone cannot deliver the 72\-hour escalation protocol, cannot produce trend charts, and cannot be audited by funders or verifiers easily\. The platform is the single point of truth that connects a CCA in a canoe to the Municipal Assembly and the Site Manager\.

Mobile app \(Android\-first, offline\-capable\): digital version of Appendix E data sheet, GPS auto\-capture, photograph attachment, offline queue, auto\-sync when in coverage\.

Webapp \(dashboard for coordinators\): live station map, per\-cluster tier indicators, alert inbox, trend charts per parameter, export to Ramsar and donor\-reporting formats\.

Integration hooks: EOSDA satellite\-derived indices \(NDVI, NDWI\) overlaid on the same map; drone orthomosaics and DSMs ingested as map layers; community photographs geotagged into the same record\.

Governance: role\-based access \(CCA, Cluster Lead, District, Coordinator, Site Manager\); full audit log; every threshold crossing preserved as an immutable record for MRV\.

__*Indicative build cost: \(≈ USD 80,000–100,000\) for MVP \(mobile \+ web \+ backend\), with ≈ GHS \(≈ USD 15,000/year hosting and maintenance thereafter\. Build time: 10–12 weeks once scoped\.*__

## 10\.4 Spatial Management Priorities \- Where to Protect, Restore, and Repair Hydrology

The baseline shows a network that cannot be managed with a single policy\. Three complementary spatial strategies are needed, drawn directly from the cluster tier ratings in Table 8\.1\. None of these should be implemented through blanket policing, they are community\-anchored management zones, not exclusion zones\.

### 10\.4\.1 Areas for Protection and Conservation

Zones where ecosystem function is already intact\. The management goal here is to keep them that way, through community stewardship, buffer maintenance, and formal recognition in Municipal/District spatial plans rather than fencing or enforcement\.

__Zone__

__Tier__

__Why protect__

__Management instruments__

Saviatula–Galotse–Agortoe–Agbastivi \(Keta Cluster 2\)

GOOD

Highest humus in network \(37\.5–40\.6%\), intact productive mangrove core, carbon engine of Keta

Community management agreement; no\-take buffer inside 50 m of standing mangrove; CCA\-led quarterly patrols

Genui Reference Station \(Keta Cluster 4\)

GOOD

High\-moisture SOC\-preserving regime; functions as the network reference point

Designate as scientific reference site; restrict mechanised access; long\-term monitoring anchor

Azizanya–Ayigbo \(Ada Cluster 6\)

GOOD

Volta\-stabilised freshwater baseline; reference for Ada\-Songor water quality

Protect freshwater inflow corridor; assembly by\-law recognising the cluster as a reference zone

Afiadenyigba–Anloga inner main\-lagoon margin \(Keta Cluster 5, healthy sub\-zones\)

MODERATE→GOOD

Carbon stocks still functional; alkalinity manageable

Community buffer agreements; monitor trend, escalate if tier slips

### 10\.4\.2 Areas for Active Restoration

Zones where ecosystem function is impaired but recoverable\. These are the priority sites for replanting, canopy re\-establishment, and nutrient rehabilitation\.

__Zone__

__Tier__

__Restoration target__

__Primary intervention__

Aborlorvi–Norlopi \(Keta Cluster 3\)

MODERATE \(restoration candidate\)

Re\-establish moisture regime and humus accumulation

Community\-led replanting \(Rhizophora \+ Avicennia mix\), boardwalk access, 3\-year moisture monitoring

Patukope–Pute \(Ada Cluster 7\)

STRESSED

Reverse soil humus depletion, stabilise pH, restore canopy cover

Replanting plus canopy shade restoration to bring soil temperature back below 35 °C; community nursery

Obane Creek \(Ada Cluster 9 sub\-zone\)

RECOVERING

Lock in the humus recovery already observed; manage P/K enrichment risk

Exclude cattle from recovering zone; continue passive restoration; sediment monitoring

Anyanui–Tunu Road soil margin \(Keta Cluster 1 sub\-zone, excl\. acid\-front\)

STRESSED

Re\-wet and replant outside the acid\-sulphate core

Phased replanting aligned to hydrological repair \(see §8\.4\.3\)

### 10\.4\.3 Areas Requiring Hydrological Intervention

Zones where the underlying problem is hydrology, freshwater starvation, tidal exchange blockage, or acid\-sulphate oxidation\. No amount of replanting will hold until the water regime is fixed\. These sites require engineering\-grade assessment before any restoration investment\.

__Zone__

__Hydrological issue__

__Evidence__

__Recommended next step__

Anyanui–Tunu Road \(Keta Cluster 1, acid\-sulphate front\)

Exposed acid\-sulphate soils, pH 2\.7

Figure 1, Figure 7; Appendix H H01, H23

Re\-wetting feasibility study; controlled flooding to cap sulphide oxidation before any replanting

Dzita hypersaline margin \(Keta Cluster 1 east\)

Salinity ≥40 ppt, conductivity >63 µS/cm

Figure 1; Appendix H H02–H04

Tidal exchange audit; identify and clear the flow restriction driving hypersaline lock\-in

Wasakuse–Togbloku \(Ada Cluster 9\)

Oxidising anomaly, ORP peak 1,547 mV; elevated soil nitrate ≈2× MRV threshold

Figure 15, Figure 18; Appendix H H39, H48

Catchment nutrient audit; investigate upstream drainage or effluent source; hydrological flushing diagnostic

Pute soil margin \(Ada Cluster 7\)

Soil pH close to critical, canopy absence raising soil temperature

Figure 17; Appendix H H41–H44

Micro\-hydrology mapping to restore lateral water movement before replanting

Keta freshwater\-to\-hypersaline gradient \(lagoon\-wide\)

Network\-scale salinity gradient driving cluster divergence

Figure 4 \(§4\.6\.2\)

Lagoon\-wide hydrological master plan integrated with the Carbon Inventory and Policy reports

__*The spatial logic is deliberate: protect what works, restore what can recover, and fix the hydrology that makes restoration possible\. Community\-led implementation through CREMAs and CCAs, supported by District and the Wildlife Division technical staff and the digital monitoring platform, is the delivery model that is consistent with both the Ramsar obligations and the scale of the network\.*__

Verra \(2021\)\. *VM0033: Methodology for Tidal Wetland and Seagrass Restoration, v2\.1*\. Verra, Washington, DC, USA\.

Wildfowl and Wetlands Trust \(WWT\) \(n\.d\.\)\. Threats to wetlands\. Retrieved from: https://www\.wwt\.org\.uk/our\-work/threats\-to\-wetlands\.

## 11\.2 Appendices

### Appendix A: Keta Lagoon Water Sampling Station Register

__Table A\.1: Water Sampling \- Sampling Sites and Description of Sites, Keta Lagoon Complex \(December 2025 Baseline\)__

This register records all 26 water quality monitoring stations at the Keta Lagoon Complex\. GPS coordinates are given in decimal\-degree format as Northing \(N\) and Easting \(E\)\. Cluster assignments follow the ecological cluster framework established in Chapter 8\. All coordinates were recorded in the field during the December 2025 assessment using handheld GPS devices\.

__Methodology note:__ Water samples were collected at each station using a calibrated multi\-parameter probe \(YSI or equivalent\)\. Parameters measured in situ include pH, temperature, conductivity, salinity, total dissolved solids \(TDS\), dissolved oxygen \(DO\), oxidation\-reduction potential \(ORP\), and resistivity\. Three readings were taken at each station and averaged\. Stations were selected to represent the full ecological gradient of the complex, from the Volta\-influenced western margin at Anyanui through the productive mangrove core at the Salo\-Galotse corridor to the main lagoon bank at Afiadenyigba\-Anloga\.

__Table C\.1: Soil Sampling \- Sampling Sites and Description of Sites, Keta Lagoon Complex \(December 2025 Baseline\)__

This register records all 20 soil quality monitoring stations at the Keta Lagoon Complex\. ### Appendix F: Ecosystem Health Condition Scoring Criteria

__Ghana Blue Carbon Assessment \- Condition Scoring Reference Card__

__THE FOUR\-TIER CONDITION CLASSIFICATION SYSTEM__

The Ghana Blue Carbon Assessment uses a four\-tier classification __GOOD__, __MODERATE__, __STRESSED__, and __ALERT__ applied independently to water quality and soil quality at each monitoring station, then integrated into a cluster\-level overall condition score\. The fourth tier \(ALERT, distinguished from the simpler three\-tier "Good/Moderate/Poor" framework\) is used because the Ghana dataset contains parameter values that represent qualitatively different ecological states \(active, irreversible carbon loss\) that cannot be managed equivalently to sites that are merely showing stress signals\.

__Any single parameter meeting an ALERT threshold is sufficient to classify the entire station as ALERT, regardless of other parameter values\.__

__Table F\.1: Water Quality Condition Scoring Criteria \- Quantitative Thresholds by Tier__

Parameter

Units

GOOD

MODERATE

STRESSED

ALERT

pH

—

6\.0–8\.0

5\.5–6\.0 or 8\.0–8\.5

< 5\.5 or > 8\.5

< 4\.0 \(any reading\)

Temperature

°C

< 30\.0

30\.0–33\.0

33\.0–35\.0

> 35\.0

Conductivity

mS/cm

Within benchmark range for cluster

Elevated above benchmark

Significantly above benchmark

Extreme; indicative of saline intrusion or pollution loading

Salinity \(Rhizophora\)

ppt

5–35

35–40 \(upper tolerance limit\)

> 40 \(hypersaline; persistent\)

Sustained hypersalinity combined with other stressors

Total Dissolved Solids \(TDS\)

ppm

Within cluster benchmark

Elevated above benchmark

Significantly above benchmark

Extreme; indicative of evaporative concentration or pollution

mg/L

> 5\.0

3\.0–5\.0

< 3\.0 \(approaching hypoxia\)

< 2\.0 \(hypoxic; fish kill risk\)

Oxidation\-Reduction Potential \(ORP\)

mV

\+300 to \+600 \(moderately oxidising\)

\+600 to \+800 \(strongly oxidising\)

> \+800 \(extremely oxidising; carbon loss risk\)

Resistivity

Declining below cluster benchmark

Extremely low; consistent with saline overload

## 11\.3 Data Provenance and Attribution

All field data in this report was collected by the Ghana Blue Carbon Assessment team during the December 2025 baseline assessment at Keta Lagoon Complex \(Keta Municipal and Anloga Districts, Volta Region\) and Ada\-Songor Lagoon \(Ada East District, Greater Accra Region\)\. Data collection was conducted under the authority of the Ghana Wildlife Division, Forestry Commission, with support from the WACA ResIP 2 programme financed through the World Bank Group \(P175525\)\.

Community Change Agents \(CCAs\) participated in all field data collection activities, receiving hands\-on training in the use of multi\-parameter water and soil probes throughout the assessment period\. 

The condition scoring framework applied in this report \(Chapter 8, summarised in Appendix F\) was developed specifically for the Ghana Blue Carbon context, drawing on the ecological parameter ranges established in the December 2025 baseline data and the tier definitions of blue carbon ecosystem function described in Howard et al\. \(2014\), Donato et al\. \(2011\), and the IPCC \(2014\) Wetlands Supplement\. Verra VM0033 alert threshold criteria \(Verra, 2021\) informed the MRV escalation thresholds adopted for soil nutrient parameters\.
